Happy Imbolc! I hope the holiday finds you and yours well.

Given your discussion of His Grace Kek and your examination of the Basement Brigade, I’m gonna step not very far out on a stout oak limb to suspect that you and the assembled wizardren are aware of a British meme goddess named Amelia.

She does seem potent, having been awarded epithets including, “Lionheart,” and “Rose of Albion.” One standout meme reads, “Amelia, Queen of Albion, stand with me now!”

While the quality of her videos and music videos on YouTube makes me suspect someone is funding a little pot-stirring, they are sincere and consistent. Also, one music video, “Amelia’s Dream,” on Music of Our Time at YouTube might hold something beyond the content creator’s intent since it features a standing stone, rooks and seagulls.

Technical stuff aside, I like her despite my Puritan and Patriot roots. Assuming that the gamers might know about her here in the US, I asked one. He said, “I’m a loyal Irishman, and I love her!” Okay. That’s not strange, not a bit…

But – I am left with a suspicion sneaking as quietly as a straight-piped bulldozer that, despite being a cartoon character who hits all the right Jungian symbols, there is something more afoot than an adorable waifu and some bumper-sticker sloganeering. (That the Guardian is reduced to foaming aphasia in their denunciations of her may support the possibility of afoot-ness.)

So0O0o-ah – Who is Amelia and what is she really doing?

replies.content:
A lot of this cognitive collapse seems to me to be caused by a lack of anything in children's culture that requires their slow, laborious and sustained concentration for any great length of time. So they start their school years without any experience of this sort of mental work.

When I was young and TV was in its tiny-screen, black-and-white infancy, shows moved from their beginnings to their ends at a measured pace, news was a person speaking thoughtfully and carefully, and in general the viewer was rarely startled. One had time to think along with the show as it moved from its beginning to its end. And most popular music was primarily melodic, with simple, regular rhythms. And very many people spent hours and hours reading books of one sort or another, books that required sustained attention to get much out of them.

Nowadays children's visual media are full of sudden "jump cuts," shifts of scene, and rapid movements. Their music seems to downplay melodic development in favor of sudden surprises. All this will produce a steady flow of dopamine "hits," which eventually becomes addictive. And it esily comes to saturate their lives

This shift (if memory serves me rightly) began in the very late 1950s, and picked up steam in the 1960s and 1970s. That's when -- and why! -- TV lost any attraction it had for me, and by the mid-1980s I had entirely stopped watching it.

But effective studying of anything is impossible without slow, laborious and sustained concentration. If children haven't already learned how to do this sort of work, they can't be taught much of anything.

By way of illustration. my wife's great-grandfather, Benjamin Randall Jordan, was a farmer's son, and worked all his life as a farmer. He had just ten months of schooling in a one-room schoolhouse in rural Maine (Parsonsfield). Yet he owned what was for his day a fairly large library of perhaps 75-100 books, whic has come down to my wife intact. It included such books as Milton's Paradise Lost, Butler's Hudibras, a textbook of astronomy with its accompanying sky atlas, a handbook of all the world's religions, a good-sized dictionary, and so forth -- all of them worn from being read over and over.

We have created a media-saturated civilization where children are handicapped by a lack of mental exercise in their young years, to the point where most of them will never ever be capable of that sort of labor with so many books. Only a fraction of the students I taught at my Ivy-League University (1967-2005) seemed capable of doing that sort of slow careful reading, and not all of them were actually willing to do it.

replies.content:
Morality is a human thing. It concerns the rules that are appropriate to human beings. The moral virtues are human virtues, just as a plant (for example) can have healing virtues. We don't criticize the morality of a lioness when she springs upon a gazelle, or call the sea evil when it drowns people, because they're not human and thus not subject to human morality.

The same is true of the gods. They are emphatically not human, and the laws by which they are governed are not human laws. (That's true of the god of Christianity, btw, as much as any other: as Amos 3:6 says, "Shall there be evil in a city, and the Lord hath not done it?") The gods are the primary powers of existence, as far outside human morality as the law of gravity. The 2000-year experiment of attempting to judge them by human standards has fared very poorly, all things considered, and it's arguably time that we stop fooling ourselves.

None of this, btw, weakens the importance of moral principles in human life. Morals and ethics are the rules we've evolved over most of a million years to help us achieve excellence and live with each other in relative peace; the fact that they don't apply to nonhuman realities doesn't keep them from applying to us. As a certain shoggoth once whistled, "the world has no eyes, but we have eyes."

replies.title: Re: Ghosts
replies.content:
Exactly. Most ghosts aren't actively predatory, and those who are, can be banished readily by a competent practitioner.

I'd be utterly delighted, btw, if "voor" came to be used as a common term for etheric energy. I didn't invent the word -- I borrowed it from Arthur Machen, as part of my general subversion of his weird tales -- but it's a good convenient label.

replies.title: Re: Ghosts

replies.title: Re: Ghosts
replies.content: That is indeed how the Dutch 'voor' is pronounced. It simply means "for" in Dutch, so I doubt Machen selected it from that language. I enjoy etymology. At any rate, I like the word and will put it to use now.

replies.title: Re: Ghosts

How would this play out if one person in a household is a competent practitioner who might attract ghosts but could easily deal with them, but other household members aren't? comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
At this page is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts (printable version here, current to 1/29). Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

* * *

May Larry Mulford, who has entered hospice after a year battling with pancreatic cancer, pass in the smoothest possible manner, and may his wife be enveloped in our love.

May Marko have the strength to seize the opportunities.

May Luke Z and his house, whose furnace has problems that can't be fixed until after the current severe weather ends, be blessed and kept safe until the cold subside.

May Pierre's young daughter, Athena, be healed from her fatigue and its root causes in ways that are easy, natural, and as holistic as possible.

May Bob Ralston (aka Rasty Bob), who is in hospice care in Buckeye AZ, and who just lost his wife Leslie Fish, be blessed and find relief from his pain and discomfort; may Bob's heart remain strong.

May Leslie Fish, wife of Bob Ralston, who passed away in early December, be blessed and make a peaceful transition to her next existence.

May Corey Benton, who passed away on 12/10, be blessed and make a peaceful transition to his next destination.

May Satoko L in Kyoto, who is recovering at home after weeks of hospitalization for Acute Hepatitis while in a state of immunodeficiency, continue to heal quickly and safely, and return to full vitality.

May 5 year old Max be blessed and protected during his parents' contentious divorce; may events work out in a manner most conducive to Max's healthy development over the long term.

May Lydia G. of Geauga County, Ohio heal and recover from prolonged health issues.

May both Monika and the child she is pregnant with both be blessed with good health and a safe delivery.

May Mary's sister have her auto-immune conditions sent into remission, may her eyes remain healthy, and may she heal in body, mind, and spirit.

May the abcess in JRuss's left armpit heal quickly.

May Brother Kornhoer's son Travis's left ureter be restored to full function, may his body have the strength to fight off infections, may his kidneys strengthen, and may his empty nose syndrome abate, so that he may have a full and healthy life ahead of him.

May HippieVikings's baby HV, who was born safely but has had some breathing concerns, be filled with good health and strength.

* * *

Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

comments.title: Charles Williams
comments.content: Maybe this is a better place to discuss Charles Williams than on the other blog where it's off-topic.  Those golden dancing figures in "The Greater Trumps": perpetual motion? replies.title: Re: Charles Williams
replies.content:
The golden dancing figures are echoing the movements of the officers in a Golden Dawn temple. Though his Christian fans don't like to admit this, Williams was a very active member of Arthur Edward Waite's branch of the Golden Dawn, and served as Hierophant (chief initiating officer) of the London temple for two six-month terms. My take is that Williams put that in partly because it's a good bit of literary color and partly as a nod and a wink to his fellow Golden Dawnies. As for the orrery in Crowley's Little, Big, other than the source of its power, it's based on real things. Here's a room-sized orrery in the Netherlands:
[IMAGE:0]

comments.title: Prayer
comments.content:
I mentioned a while back that I had developed a WOH-adjacent devotional ritual, by adding a water step to the ritual that Jenny learned in Kingsport, using potpourri and candles.

The method I developed and use is as follows:
1. [dip fingers in blessed water, draw symbol on forehead] “With this water I am purified.”
2. [take scent sticks and wave over altar, then sniff deeply] “With this scent I am attuned.”
3. [light candles} “With this light I approach the deity, saying......”

Well, I recently received a copy of a book of introductory readings in Neoplatonic Philosophy (which your writings have inspired me to do). And I was struck by the advice found in Iamblichus's advice on prayer (On The Mysteries of the Egyptians)... I was instantly attracted to two passages which outline a three-step process with which to approach divinity (bullet pointing by me):

[re: the theurgic “gateway to the creator of all things”]

“In the first place, it has the power to purify the soul, far more perfect than the power to purify the body...
“...afterwards, it prepares the mind for the participation in and vision of the Good and for a release from everything that opposes it and...
“...at the last, for a union with the gods who are givers of all things good.”

[re: prayer]
“I declare then, that the first degree of prayer is the introductory, which leads to contact and acquaintance with the divine.
“The second is conjunctive, producing a union of sympathetic minds and calling forth benefactions sent down by the gods even before we think of them.
“The most perfect, finally, has as its mark ineffable unification, which establishes all authority in the gods and provides that our souls rest completely in them.”

Somehow, dare I believe, that my wee crayon scribble of a three-step ritual – purification, attunement, and approach - contains an echo of something much older and deeper?

comments.title: On exercising the will
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

One of your comments last week about training the will got me to thinking about my own mixed efforts in that regard, and the reasons for failure. One consistent reason is that there are always good reasons to change one's mind and abandon the previous commitment.

There's a lot of words for people who stick by their will and refuse to change in the face of changing circumstances: obstinate, irrational, unreasonable, insane... I like to think of myself as a rational, smart, reasonable person and this seems to conflict with choosing a path and sticking with it simply because it's the one I chose. Most of the famous people we admire for their willpower were following their will in cases where the reasonable, smart thing to do was give in.

If there's anyone else out there who sees themselves as rational, smart and reasonable, you might want to check whether that self-image is undermining your strength of will. I've certainly had a bit more success since I embraced the idea that "Yes, this is irrational and unreasonable, I'm doing it anyway."

Thank you for your help.

replies.title: Re: On exercising the will
replies.content: That's an excellent point. There are always good reasons to change your mind; this is why it's best to start will training with exercises that are completely pointless, except as will training. When you put up a sticky note by your desk that reads "Touch your nose," and you touch your nose with your left hand every time you notice it, it's a completely pointless act, so arguments against doing it mean nothing -- and that helps you build the habit of doing what you will.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I'm glad to hear that your move has been successful. I've got a few questions now that you're back:

1) In Israel Regardie's The Golden Dawn, the LBRP pentagram is drawn clockwise, starting at the bottom, and the LIRP pentagram counter-clockwise from the top. In The Heathen Golden Dawn, both start at the top and the directions are reversed, the LBRP being counter-clockwise and the LIRP clockwise. Is this a change that you have made in the CGD and was included in the HeGD or is this HeGD specific? If it's the former (or the latter and you know the rational), why?

2) In Monsters, you say that etheric energies can act almost like solid matter if concentrated. Is this how frustrated teenagers can cause poltergeist phenomena?

3) Is it possible to learn how to do this intentionally? Not just causing a ruckus, I mean, but to concentrate etheric energies to remotely manipulate objects (telekinesis, I suppose)?

4) Unbelievable as it sounds, I was hagged just two days after reading about Hag attacks in Monsters. I first thought that it was just a plain old nightmare but divination confirmed that this was not the case. It seems to have been an entirely different phenomenon from the cat-thing I mentioned a couple of months back as this was the first time I heard the sandpaper footsteps. An astral LBPR stopped the attack, presumably before any damage was done as the day afterwards, I only felt the ordinary exhaustion of having slept a few hours too little (there was no way I was going back to bed after that).

What bothers me is that I have a salt jar on my nightstand and it still looks perfectly fine. If Hag attacks are etheric phenomena, shouldn't the jar have prevented it?

5) Regarding the theory that fae food could be toadstool in disguise, hence people being unable to return from Faery once they have eaten it, I was under the impression that healthy humans are able to consume toadstool, even if it's very unpleasant? Amanita muscaria is an entheogen, after all.

—David P.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The classic GD pentagram ritual is strongly influenced by Christianity, so what you banish is the element of Earth -- "the world, the flesh, and the devil." The Celtic GD ritual, being Druidical in its focus, doesn't do this; it invokes by tracing a clockwise descent from spirit through the elements, and banishes by tracing a counterclockwise reabsorption of the elements into spirit. The Heathen GD follows the latter pattern.

2) Yes.

3) Yes, but if you think I'm going to explain how in a public forum like this, think again.

4) The salt jar absorbs hostile spells. If you have further problems with hagging, you want to put a dish of vinegar somewhere in your bedroom. The acid vapor will prevent the spirit from maintaining a dense etheric body.

5) This is the kind of casual assumption that kills people. Amanita muscaria can drop you stone cold dead if you misjudge the dose -- that's why Siberian shamans fed it to reindeer and drank the reindeers' urine, which diluted the active constituents enough to make it somewhat safe. Many other toadstools are lethal poisons, which cause sudden total liver failure and the like. You can find all this out in tens of thousands of readily available sources online and off. Please be a little more wary!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you.

3) Fair enough. I was more interested in knowing whether it's possible at all anyway, seeing as I'm far from ready to try anything that ambitious. I suspect that when I am, I'll have a rough idea how to find more information on the matter.

5) "other toadstools"—oh, the joys of learning English in a German school! We were (and presumably are) still taught many false definitions, such as tortoise being a synonym of turtle. This appears to be another such case. I was under the impression that toadstool was a name for Amanita muscaria, rather than poisonous mushrooms in general.

As for A. muscaria, noted. I would have done more research if I had planned to actually eat it.

—David P.

replies.title: Amanita Muscaria
replies.content:
I think you're overblowing the toxic affects of Amanita muscaria. https://www.amanitadreamer.net/ I have this woman's first book, have attended her talks both in person and online, and have used the mushroom myself. While it can make you quite sick if you take too much, it's not in the same class as the Destroying Angel (Amanita virosa, Amanita bisporigera, and Amanita ocreata) all of which destroy your liver over a period of a few days because they contain a toxin that your liver can't break down.

With regard to Amanita muscaria, the main reason Siberian shamans used the reindeer to process the mushroom was to achieve full muscimol conversion, which significantly increases the hallucinogenic effects of the mushroom. Without full conversion, which can be achieved in your kitchen without a reindeer, you end up with a lot more ibogaine in the tea you make from it. This is what will cause the gastric distress. Some people do go to the hospital for this but the effects are treatable. Whereas by the time you go to the hospital for the effects of one of the Destroying Angels, it will be too late because there is no cure.

FSHA

replies.title: Re: Amanita Muscaria
replies.content: It's entirely possible that I was misinformed, but I notice from a quick glance that there are some reports of deaths from A. muscaria consumption in online medical literature. I'm aware of the distinction between A. muscaria and the hepatotoxic species in the genus, however.

comments.title: To ask - to give
comments.content:
To ask:
Recently I tried restarting The Earth Mysteries Workbook, because I`m still faszinated by th old straight tracks and them going back ages of ages. In perusing the bibliography I´ve jumped to the conculsion that not some kind of ...physical force... sort of energiezes the ones that could be active in such ways but an entity of some kind. Is that a misconception, or partly maybe right, or something else?

To give
1) About jumping to conclusions:
Apparently there is a megalithic site/something in the area here and in that place nearby (separated by a highway (Autobahn) there is a street called "Leppsteinwiesen". In Austria (Eastern Tirolia) there is a mountain that is called "Leppleskofl" (...kofl apparentely being the local word for mountain) and I´ve jumped to the conclusion that maybe I should find out what kind of stone could come from the Leppleskofl and find out if that is a material interesting for the stoneage (or earlier ;-).

2) A few years ago I wondered if my life made any sense or was worth anything at all. This surfaced while kids and other adults were playing at a kind of fountain hereabouts where local people come to get water. I stared depressedly into a hedge.
After some time of doing that my mind reported back that I actually was seeing something, I put out my hand (I was squatting) closed it around that object and it appeared to be a semiprecious grey stone (Halbedelstein). Probably it fell from a car of one of the people fetching water. First I wondered if I could somehow give it back - but there was no way that made sense to me. It felt as if the stone had come to (for?!) me.
Then I decided it was an Omen and interpreted it as: no my life might not make sense and have no meaning at all, still it is precious (at least semi... ;-) I felt a kind of awe that in that precise moment of down-in-the-moutness that stone would come to me. I´m still very thankful for it.

replies.title: Re: To ask - to give
replies.content:
1) That's a common interpretation among students of the old straight tracks. We know so little at this point that it's impossible to tell whether it's correct or not.

2) Interesting! It could be anything from the early Stone Age to the Middle Ages -- mountain quarries with good stone were well known to the early Freemasons who built the big cathedrals, after all.

3) Definitely an omen. Just remember that life doesn't have to make sense to us -- human beings aren't that smart, really. Doubtless there's a purpose to it all, known to the gods.

replies.title: Re: To ask - to give
replies.content: Thank you, and a short update: a Läppstein is a stone much like a grinding stone, so probably more the middle ages

replies.title: Re: To ask - to give
replies.content: Grindstones became very valuable in the Neolithic, since that's what you used to put a polish on the stone tools of the time, and remained valuable straight through the  Bronze and Iron ages for similar reasons. So it may be quite ancient!

comments.title: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
comments.content:
Dear JMG,   thanks for taking the time to host another Magic Monday, despite the circumstances. I hope that these will normalize for you soon.

Recently I have read up on and intensified my Hellenic polytheist/theurgic practice alongside the practice of the GSF material.

I know I have to be careful with the way I phrase this question as will become clearer further below - but as far as I am aware something similar has not yet been asked and should provide some insight for those interested.

  I have started reciting the Orphic Hymns corresponding to the deities of the weekdays and thought about starting this little ritual with the first Hymn “Orpheus to Mousaios” as in my view it adds a whole other dimension to invoke and honor essentially the whole pantheon first.   However, in Patrick Dunn’s translation there is this line in the first hymn:

“I call on (…) the most holy daimon, and the daimon baneful to mortals too, and the daimons of heaven, of the air, in the water, and the earth, under the earth, and coursing through fire”.

This line is much more explicit in who one is calling on than Taylor is in his translation which made me reconsider the idea of reciting this hymn, especially on a regular basis. I have no troubles invoking the agathos daimon or the elemental spirits (that’s part of the SoP anyways) - but an invitation for baneful spirits - even in the hierarchy and company of the rest of the pantheon might not be all that smart.   

1) What would you advise? How would you categorize this kind of working considering the presence of children under the age of seven in the home?   I am intrigued but I don’t want to end up like the Sorcerer’s Apprentice - especially insofar as other people might get affected.

2) If that hymn is used wouldn’t it be appropriate or even necessary to add a “license to depart” or some other formula of dismissal? There doesn’t seem to be one supplied in the Orphic material.

3) On a tangential note: Is the use of the corresponding essential oils in a burner an adequate replacement for the burning of incense for the weekday invocations? (I’ve tried different configurations but the smoke alarm is not happy with the fumigation and cannot otherwise be mitigated.)

I might be overly cautious here, but I figured it’s best to seek counsel if it is so graciously offered.  

Thank you very much!

  -Florian

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content:
1) This is why I always use the Thomas Taylor translations. He was a practitioner, not just a scholar -- he worshipped the old gods, and adapted the old hymns intelligently to fit modern conditions. I also don't recommend the Hymn to Musaeus in a household setting, precisely because it invokes some fairly perilous powers -- my sense is that back in the day, it was used exclusively in mystery temples.

2) You can't banish a god, or even give one license to depart. Once you invoke them, there they are. Thus it's important to be careful whom you invoke.

3) You can certainly use essential oils in a burner. I've taken to using herbal potpourris myself.

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content:
Thank you for confirming my suspicions in this matter. ("I have a bad feeling about this.")

I will postpone the use of that hymn until the right circumstances in my life should arrive (i.e. within the context of an appropriately working order/mystery tradition dedicated to such matters in the proper ritual space under the guidance of experienced adepts).

For the time being I will stick to the regular weekday invocations - and to that of Athena.

As a follow up question: Which weekday would be appropriate for the recitation of the Orphic Hymn to Athena? Tuesday because of the Martian connection? Or Wednesday because of the Mercurial attributes of Athena? Or does the day matter at all?

And a note on the Dunn translation: I found it a bit odd, to be honest, as it is subtitled "A new translation for the occult practitioner". It seems the translation is quite literal and thus far less suited to ritual practice than is Taylor's. It's interesting and enlightening to read this new translation, sure. But for practical use I would not recommend it. This surprised me as Dunn himself is a practitioner. He also wrote The Practical Art of Divine Magic which I find an excellent resource.

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content:
Athena's not a planetary goddess, so can be invoked any time you like.

My use of the Orphic hymns is (of course!) idiosyncratic. I've written three hymns in the same meter, using large chunks of Taylor where appropriate, for the three Druid deities Hu the Mighty, Ced the Earth-mother, and Hesus. I recite these daily, followed by the hymn for the planet corresponding to the day, and then the hymn to the Genius or Daimon. The results have been consistently very good.

I mention this partly to indicate that I've had excellent practical results with Taylor's hymns, and partly to suggest that there's a lot of wiggle room in this kind of work.

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content:
Thank you very much.

I will work toward some similar arrangement of the hymns for daily practice and will rely on my own experimentation.

So far I have recited the hymns before going over to the SoP, purely based on my intuition. I could come up with a variety of reasons for any order. Would you have any recommendation?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content: I do it the same way -- I make offerings (wheaten cakes, fresh water, and the scent of a forest potpourri), recite the hymns as prayer, and then do my ritual and meditation practice. They seem to be well satisfied with that.

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content: I have Dunn's book as well as Taylor's and the scholarly translation by Apostolos Athanassakis. I've been doing what you're doing for years, that is, reciting the appropriate hymn for the day, and I've been using the "AA" translation. I felt that Taylor jiggered stuff around to fit the hymns into a meter, and so I preferred to use the "literal" words, but I've come to feel that AA is a little dead and dry. Dunn's is much more lively. I should try the Taylor version and see how that works. I can tell you that I much prefer Dunn's version of the Hymn to Hekate to the others (I use this one on New Moon days). That said, Dunn's book is for the occult practitioner, I think, because he includes a lot of practical things people can do with the hymns based on his practice in Wicca. I'm not making use of these things myself, but I consider his translation very valuable as an alternate, but still quite literal, reading of the hymns. But, as is frequently said here, your mileage may vary...

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content:
Re No 3, another option is the one I've been using, called fragrance sticks or reed diffusers.

These are absorbent wick-like sticks which permanently sit in a "bath" of a base oil (eg sweet almond oil), which you refresh from time to time with your chosen essential oil.

replies.title: Re: Reciting the first Orphic Hymn "Orpheus to Mousaios"
replies.content:
Thank you for that recommendation.

I might use this method for my smaller altar dedicated to Hestia - since I won't have to change the scents around daily. For the daily recitations I will likely switch to using a candle powered oil burner.

replies.title: Re: Astrological correspondences of plants and herbs
replies.content:
I cannot say anything about stones, but certainly the herbal offerings of Nicholas Culpeper (early 1600's) assigned astrological correspondences to every herb discussed.

(one of his works is here: https://www.gutenberg.org/files/49513/49513-h/49513-h.htm )

Culpeper was a "working person's" herbalist, in that he was interested in giving people more understanding of and power over their own health. His view of the apothecary trade (to which he had been apprenticed) was that it was too focussed on its own mystique, and insufficiently interested in enabling people to learn to manage health matters for themselves. :)

replies.title: Re: Astrological correspondences of plants and herbs
replies.content:
I just finished a book called "The Zodiac Garden" which will hopefully be out in April, and goes into all this, particularly in the scope of astrological gardening, but here are some of my favorite sources:
Nicholas Culpeper's 'The English Physician', 'The Picatrix' (JMG & Chris Warnocks translation is great), Agrippa's 'Three Books of Occult Philosophy' (Eric Purdue translation), Lilly's 'Christian Astrology', Joseph Blagrave's 'Astrological Practice of Physick', and the modern "The Green Mysteries" by Daniel Shulke. These are more focused on herbal correspondences, but some of them have stones too. There are some great traditional lapidaries around as well, such as 'The Lapidary of King Alphonso X', and Albertus Magnus's 'Book of Minerals'. I would not really recommend "Magical Herbalism" by Scott Cunningham.

comments.title: Time and Distance
comments.content:
Well, I bought your book (mundane Astrology, and unfortunately quite a few more) and I am working my way through the process algorithms of the discipline. I haven't come to any conclusion yet, but this far, I don't see any huge problems.

But I was wondering, and I can't seem to find anything that addresses this, so I will ask here:

Is there any school of astrology that takes into account the actual distance between earth and the other planets (inferior and superior conjunctions) when building a chart?

Thanks in advance to everyone who wants to swing at this.

replies.title: Re: Time and Distance
replies.content: I have never encountered one that does.

replies.title: Re: Time and Distance
replies.content:
I wouldn't expect exact distance to be significant. The astral light, which in some schools of thought is the source of astrology's influence, works primarily on the astral plane, where distance is best thought of as a suggestion.

(Think about how distance in dreams works: there's usually a clear separation of *here* and *there* but a *there* that seems very far might be quicker and easier to reach than one that seems very near, even accounting for obstacles.)

That said, there are two factors in astrology that are significant and which correlate closely with distance: first, the planet's speed in the sky (closer = faster = more obvious but shorter-lasting influence vs. farther = slower = more subtle but longer-lasting influence), and second, the fact that Saturn is the farthest planet ordinarily visible to the naked eye.

Because of this, Saturn corresponds to natural limits, and many of the things ruled by Uranus and Neptune are about going beyond those natural limits through various ways of shifting the cost of doing so onto something or someone else.

(Uranus is *occasionally* visible to the naked eye but hard to recognize as a planet, which makes quite a bit of sense of the status of some prominent Uranian phenomena throughout history.)

comments.title: The Outer Planets and Overcoming Limits
comments.content:
In my response to Deringoglade re: astrology something hit me: the planets beyond Saturn have as one of their central themes overcoming the usual limits imposed by the natural world. I'm not very good with astrology, though, so I figure it would help to run the idea by those who are.

Uranus does this through ruling technology and exceptions to the normal rules: less an outright defiance of the natural order than finding the loopholes in that order. (As I said, Uranus is occasionally visible, and his ruled phenomena occasionally pop up their heads in history.) However, pushed too far these loopholes begin to tear the fabric apart, imposing a cost on the future in terms of depleted resources and degraded institutions. (ex: fossil fuels)

Neptune overcomes natural limitations through playing pretend: either through building escapist fantasy worlds with different sets of limits, or manipulating collective consciousness to get others to bear the cost of overcoming the limits you don't like. (ex: the dreams of running the grid on nuclear fusion or renewables, as well as the impractical but heavily-subsidized renewables industry as actually implemented)

Pluto, which hasn't quite faded into irrelevancy, overcomes natural limitations through sheer perversity: just do what you want and weave a tale where the costs are really benefits and other people should really praise you for harming yourself and them, because reasons. (Example: radium)

replies.title: Re: The Outer Planets and Overcoming Limits
replies.content: Hmm! That certainly seems valid.

comments.title: "The Worst Argument against Paganism" and Questions on Phase 17 of "A Vision"
comments.content:
Good Afternoon,

Hope everyone is doing well, and if you marked it, have had a happy Imbolc and/or Groundhog Day!

To Share: I found this a rather strange way to get around the idea that traditions have to be contiguous to be legitimate or worthwhile, but it's a subject some here might find interesting: https://imperiumpress.substack.com/p/the-worst-argument-against-paganism

To Ask: Feel free to tell me to save this for the relevant book club post, but I've read ahead through the descriptions of the phases in an attempt to identify my own, and after some consideration, I suspect that I'm in Phase 17 (though I'm worried about the hubris of classing myself with Shelley, Dante, and Yeats!). What really resonated, though, was "Enforced Loss" as the Body of Fate: the last few years have enforced quite a bit of loss in the material realm. This leads to two questions: 1. Is the Body of Fate related to, or identical with, karma? I had been interpreting the seemingly unfortunate turns as karmic culmination, A Vision suggests it's my Body of Fate manifesting, and I'm wondering if it might be both. 2. Yeats says that for Phase 17 to live in phase, he needs to seek "simplification through intensity," and I'm hoping that if I can pursue that, it might mitigate some of pain from all of this Enforced Loss (though, if I'm understanding correctly, it doesn't seem to be something you can, or ultimately should want to, fully escape). Any tips on how to identify the true image to pursue intensely, or is the whole point of the exercise working it out for yourself?

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: "The Worst Argument against Paganism" and Questions on Phase 17 of "A Vision"
replies.content:
1) Fascinating. I think that's the first time I've ever seen the word "anti-authoritarian" used as a condemnation. I would say that the bad argument he critiques is just as vulnerable from within the belief in individual autonomy he abhors. If the gods are real, then the gods are real, and if they choose to rekindle their worship in this day and age, it's not exactly relevant if a bunch of human naysayers run around waving their hands and shouting "No! No!" Historical Pagan worship wasn't predicated on blind obedience to the gods, but on an exchange of gifts (Do ut des in Latin, "I give that you may give") and the establishment of bonds of personal affection and loyalty, just like healthy relationships among humans.

2) The whole point of the exercise is working it out for yourself. On the other hand, it wouldn't surprise me at all if you were Phase 17, since that phase has a certain characteristic brashness that you've demonstrated a few times! The Body of Fate is not karma per se, it's a structure that shapes and organizes karma so that you have the experiences you need in this phase. As for mitigating the Body of Fate by pursuing the true Mask, it does seem to work to some extent: Yeats had no shortage of losses, but he also achieved more than his share of triumphs, including a relatively happy marriage (arguably the least likely outcome for a man with his hangups).

This has been happening with deities in prayer and the SOP, too. Get some vitality and it goes away. Come back, goes away.

So I started experimenting with different pantheons. And no pantheons. Greek, Welsh, Irish, Norse, or just elements.

Gods would drop in. Then go away. Didn't feel like I made anyone angry. They just slipped into the background or went on vacation. I don't know. Maybe, working with the gods was not needed in this incarnation?

Happening at the same time I continued working with some of the ideas in Robert Mathiesen's books. Emptying and uniting in particular.

What I found in that work was I could de-label everything in my reality. Clouds, garbage cans, sailboats, weather, and whatever else. I thought this was leading me to glimpses of non-duality. Now I am wondering if something else is happening.

The glimpses seem like they could be of energy that might be called meaning. Another label of course.

Which takes me to the next idea. There is a power for lack of a better word in removing labels and there is a power in the act of labeling.

Back to divination and deities and I guess everything else.

I think all this struggle has been in part about learning to use labels as a tool instead of being ruled by them.

As I feel into the energies, fields and such of a tarot card or planet or zodiac sign or hexagram or rune I can begin to use their names and definitions as a container of sorts. Or a guide?

The name isn't the thing! Or the experience. Or the energy. Funny, as I first encountered this in the 90s at Wilderness Awareness School while learning about plants and animal tracking. Been working at this lesson for a very long time, I guess.

Tricky thing this labeling habit.

Heck it was even showing up in picking domain names for my blog! So many domain names. Think I finally have one. Maybe even a label for this magic I am participating with, too. Or a book!

As mentioned earlier, in prior comments this week, might I finally have found my career in this life? Being one of those old men at the end of the lane? Whispering prayers, reading signs, gazing at the moon and stars to glimpse what unfolds next in life. Seeing as it's taken me 60 years to get to this point the timing might work out just right!

Once again I appear to not have a question. Which is fitting. I woke up one morning last week with the sense that I know nothing. What a trip incarnating into this magical world!

Thanks for reading,
Eric

comments.title: A whole lotta questions
comments.content:
1. I had a last minute panic attack about a volunteer gig (deconstruction job) and did a geomancy reading about whether I should go. LW Cauda Draconis RW Puer Judge Fortuna Major. My read was "probably not (Cauda Draconis + Puer), but if you do it'll put some hair on your chest." (Puer + Fortuna Major)
My first question: without any qualifying info, is this a good read?
Anyway, I did end up going and enjoyed myself far more than my reading made me expect. Everybody there was great, good spirits and better patience. Working with them also made me realise just out of shape I am. For the first time in my life I'm going to the gym. If had accepted the strict reading "2 bad, 1 good, don't do it" I wouldn't have had so much fun and learned about myself in the process.
2. I've been studying electrical physics as well as occult philosophy over the past year and some interesting thoughts have crossed my mind. Physicists study natural phenomenon so that can use it towards some physical end. Magicians study inner, emotional phenomenon so that they can be used towards some physical end. One could call magic "Spiritual Physics!" I connected this to my new gym habit. In the past, my dreams of looking good underlay my desire to get in shape, but those never materialized. But the guilt of realising just how out of shape and my desire to keep up sent me right to the gym, no questions asked. And the excitement to go back still persists. In a "spiritual physics" frame, guilt/shame is like "Locked Rotor Current", it takes a lot more energy to move a rotor that is at a standstill. Similarly shame and guilt are really good at giving an ambitionless couch potato a kick in the butt. An apocalyptic reveal of your mistakes is really good at that. And to continue the metaphor, if a circuit maintains Locked Rotor Current for too long you pop a fuse. Similarly, guilt not properly channeled can re-paralyze you. Vanity on the other hand is low current. If you have an established routine or friends to go with, it can do just fine. But by itself, I think it's insufficient.

I think this "spiritual physics" idea could be an interesting way to meditate on emotions. Document each emotions' strength, weakness, and best use cases. Metaphor seems to be a big driver of new discoveries, and applying the metaphors of electricity to magic could be fertile indeed!

3. Something has changed in the air, I feel wired. If I had to describe the energy of the year going forward, the comparison I'd use is mountain biking. In mountain biking, it's all momentum. The most you can is slightly nudge the handlebars and hope for the best. If you ever even think of going for the brakes, it's over. I think this year is gonna be the same. People that handle momentum well will go incredibly far, but those that panic are done for. Maybe it's that Saturn-in-Aries energy. I guess my last question for you is how have the vibes been on your end. Anything perceptible beyond the hustle and bustle of moving states?

As always, thanks for your time.
Sean-Luc

comments.title: Ninja 5th dan sword strike test
comments.content:
JMG,

My question is basically what you think is going on below from a magical perspective, if you could speculate and whether you think it is possible to genuinely do what is described below.

I read in a book that the 5th dan (mastery) test in a particular tradition of Japanese ninjutsu is that the aspirant sits in seiza and meditating with eyes closed and the grandmaster stands behind him, also with eyes closed, in meditation, holding a sword (traditionally a live sword but in the last few decades more often a bamboo shinai), and the test is essentially whether you can do a forward roll out of the way of the shinai before you get smacked.

(The book is called Path Notes of an American Ninja Master by Glenn Morris and has a detailed description of his godan test in 1990)

Depending on the sensei, you may or may not get 3 chances to succeed if you get smacked.

Note that you cannot wait to hear the sword/arm/breeze move to start the roll - you will be far too slow to escape a full speed cut from that close. If you roll too early (before there is a committed stroke), you fail. In theory the sensei could swing slowly enough to let you hear the stroke and move but this is done in front of a large crowd of other senior instructors and martial artists and the sensei himself would be disgraced by doing that.

It sounds crazy (and no doubt would be the subject of debunking these days by people who fight MMA etc) but it apparently it did happen even well into this generation and probably does still happen somewhere.

Basically the only way to do it, according to the book is that you have to be able to sense the sensei's intent through his energy and there is a fraction of a second where he has made up his mind and mentally committed to the stroke but hasn't physically started moving - if you can sense that moment, you can start moving just barely in time to dodge the stroke.

The author says when he did his test, he got into seiza and reached out with his feelings to "connect" with the energy of the sensei behind him and then started to get nervous because although he could normally do this easily, the sensei behind him was in mushin and to the student's senses it felt like no one was behind him. Nevertheless he waited for the stroke.

He says he just looked at the white light he could see in the bright room with his eyes closed and saw a flicker of light that was barely there and rolled and the sword swung and missed him - he'd passed!

But no - the sensei said "Come back, do it again and this time don't think". He tried again and this time he didn't sense anything at all and got sharply cracked on the skull. Strike two.

Apparently at this point the sensei muttered something in Japanese which the student was much later told meant "These intellectuals always think too much".

Final chance. The student remembered someone else telling him that when he passed the godan test, he had a feeling of great nausea in his stomach and then rolling without any conscious thought and passing the test. He then says he "dropped my consciousness to my hara, my intestines began to roil, I shut off my [conscious] mental processes and waited and waited and suddenly I was at the other end of the room, with no memory or awareness of the roll that got me out of the way of the strike and gave me my godan rank".

Later one of the other teachers told him that from what he could see, the student had actually rolled at the right time on the first try, and he didn't know why the sensei had made him do it again until he could do it in that unconscious way. And the student says he's still not sure exactly why but the best he can guess is that "it is not enough to sense the killing thought..one must actually avoid the blow...one may be threatened by another's intent but if you react merely to the intent, then a skilled opponent can use that against you by not following through". He then says "In this test, there is an exchange of subtle energy between the sensei and initiate..the stronger spirit wins..there is a transfer of chi as part of the test".

And finally he goes on to say that when he "checked his spirits in the mirror that night" (whatever that means), he saw a new spirit had joined him - a tengu, one of the spirits that works with and is friendly to his school of Ninjutsu, a symbol of the spirits' protection and his acceptance into the tradition.

JMG - I know I've written a lot but I thought the details were quite important and I can't make out what on earth went on in this process (assuming it is all true).

replies.title: Re: Ninja 5th dan sword strike test
replies.content: Yes, this kind of thing happens. In taijiquan, the ability to "listen" to the other person's qi (life force) and be aware of what he's going to do before he does it is a normal skill developed in training -- that's much of what push-hands exercises are about. As for tengu, of course they exist -- they're one of many, many kinds of nonhuman spirit in this end of the cosmos, and ninjutsu practitioners (and the yamabushi and shugenja ascetics whose teachings helped shape ninjutsu) have long had cordial relations with them.

It's all very reminiscent of Euripides' The Bacchae, and may just end the same way.

comments.title: Prognostication
comments.content: Congratulations on your recent move. It just so happens I'm contemplating a move myself in the near future, but I haven't made a firm decision, and wanted to know what occult tools I could employ in prognosticating the results of such a life change. I intend to perform an Ogham divination, of which I have tolerable experience, but aside from that I have very little experience with other forms of divination, especially the astrological kind, and I would like more than a simple Ogham reading to help guide me on such a big change. What might you suggest for a relatively inexperienced mage? Or, if it's not too personal, what if anything did you use to guide your own recent decision to move house?

replies.title: Re: Prognostication
replies.content: I started practicing divination more than half a century ago, and one result is that my intuitive senses are fairly well developed. I use horary and relocational astrology to check my intuitions, but most of what I do is pure intuition without the intervention of divinatory symbolism; unless you're unusually talented, you may not be able to do that. What I'd recommend is that you go to a diviner who has no personal stake in your decision and see what they have to say.

comments.title: Esoteric meaning of DC's design

Later I found out that most of DC Was built according to masonic symbolism, making wonder as to the true meaning of the star. There's also a missing section of the street star, with fumors of an underground masonic tunnel connecting the street sections. Any knowledge of any of this? Gps and phone signal does become wonky insode this area - though it may just be signal blocking around the white house for entirely sensible and mundane security reasons.

The Washington Monument, aka the grand obelisk in the midst of Dc, is by law the tallest building in the city. What, from a masonice perspective, is the significance of a central obelisk that stands between the branches of government, at the central corssroads of the city, above everything else in the city?

comments.title: Unintentional Electional Astrology?
comments.content:
I have a general question and a specific one about the upcoming Saturn-Neptune conjunction. Feel free to defer either or both if you'll be addressing my concerns on your Patreon/SubscribeStar next week.

  1. The general question: You've said the conjunction is expressly not a good time for elections or astrological magic, at least for the vast majority of people. But suppose you're not planning on doing astrological magic or intentional elections — does any major action undertaken during the time of a major astrological event's influence receive the same influence as if it were elected, or does it receive only a general "vibe" insofar as the event affects/predicts everything?

  2. The specific question: Are there any particular types of actions that are counterindicated by the upcoming conjunction and better put off until later? I mean things like buying a new home, starting a business, taking up a new spiritual practice, etc. Obviously I'd ideally want a competent astrologer to look at how the conjunction interacts with my own chart, but I'm wondering if maybe there are general indications — something on the level of "avoid signing new contracts when Mercury is retrograde."

replies.title: Re: Unintentional Electional Astrology?
replies.content:
1) Any major action that takes place, or is begun, during the aspect is subject to the influence of the aspect. Since both planets are slow-moving and have been close together for a while now -- right now, for example, they're only 1 degree 23 minutes of arc apart, which is within orb by any measure -- there's a period of several months in which every action will be influenced by them, subject only to house placement. So it's already here, in effect, and can't be avoided.

2) I've been looking for those, but so far haven't found any documentation. What's clear to me at this point is that relying on existing forms to remain intact during this conjunction is a bad idea! Neptune is dissolving Saturn's rigid structures, and Saturn will establish new and unfamiliar structures once the conjunction is past. I would avoid making firm commitments during this time; at the same time, it's a good opportunity to make significant changes (such as, say, relocating) with the hope of heading in new directions.

replies.title: Re: Unintentional Electional Astrology?
replies.content: 2. Oof. That doesn't bode well for existing institutions in the United States, e.g. academia, the media, political parties, the government, corporations, etc. Probably even America's own mythology and its conception of itself and the world is going to get dissolved and rebuilt in unfamiliar form.
